meta.title: W214 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W214 engine — M254 MHEV and PHEV, OM654 diesel, and M256 AMG. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class W214 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W214 engine typically costs £2,500–£15,000 supply only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine — the volume OM654 diesel sits at £3,000–£5,500. Fitting adds 10–20 hours labour at specialist rates. PHEV battery replacement outside warranty could add £5,000–£10,000 — factor this into your ownership maths. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W214 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.820 (E 220 d, MHEV) — a 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel with 197 bhp, 440 Nm of torque, and proven reliability from the W213 generation. The M254.920 (E 200, MHEV) is the best petrol option — smooth, responsive, and with the simplest electrified system in the range.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class W214?

acceptedAnswer.text: 48V ISG and cooling system issues (MHEV variants), PHEV battery degradation and cooling system failures (PHEV variants), OM654 rear timing chain wear (projected on high‑mileage diesels), and — tech‑related — MBUX Superscreen software glitches and connectivity issues on early cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class W214 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's too early to say definitively — the W214 launched in 2023 and long‑term reliability data is limited. Early indications from the W213 suggest the M254 and OM654 are solid, but the 48V and PHEV systems add complexity. The biggest unknown is the 25.4 kWh battery — an £8,000–£15,000 replacement outside warranty is a serious consideration. Buy with a full Mercedes service history and ensure software updates are current.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W214 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a PHEV without a documented battery health check is the one we'd approach cautiously. The E 400 e and E 53 HYBRID are complex, high‑performance systems with significant potential failure points. For private buyers, the simpler MHEV variants (E 200, E 220 d) are the safer bet.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W214 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: EML or 48V system warnings (MHEV), range reduction or charging issues (PHEV battery degradation), rattling on start‑up (OM654 timing chain), rough running or misfire (M254 cooling/ISG issues). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class W214 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and whether the PHEV battery is involved. Against a well‑kept £40,000 W214, a £3,000–£5,500 OM654 recon is 7.5–14% of the car's value — safe. Even an £8,000–£15,000 M256 AMG recon is 20–38% — safe. But if the £5,000–£10,000 PHEV battery needs replacing at the same time, the total could exceed 30–60% of the car's value — evaluate carefully. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W214 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M254 petrol can reach 150,000+ miles with 48V system maintenance. The PHEV battery is rated for 8 years / 100,000 miles — beyond that, degradation is expected. The M256 straight‑six is robust but complex — 100,000+ miles is achievable with meticulous servicing.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W214, S214 and X214 E-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the sixth‑generation E‑Class platform and all engines. The W214 is the 4‑door saloon (this page), the S214 is the 5‑door estate, and the X214 is the All‑Terrain estate with raised suspension and off‑road styling. The estate and All‑Terrain have slightly higher kerb weights and fuel consumption — running costs are marginally higher than the saloon.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W214 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation; also accessible via the MBUX infotainment system (Vehicle → Engine information). Tell us your variant, registration year and whether it's MHEV or PHEV and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class W214 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes E-Class W214 — from the 2.0‑litre M254 mild‑hybrid petrol to the 3.0‑litre M256 straight‑six AMG, plus the OM654 diesel and the new‑generation plug‑in hybrids with over 70 miles of electric range. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the first E‑Class to go all‑electrified. Backed by 276+ real UK W214 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class W214 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W214 specifically — from the entry‑level 1.5‑litre M254 to the 3.0‑litre M256 straight‑six in the AMG E 53, all with 48V mild‑hybrid or plug‑in hybrid assistance.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All seven engine families are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any of these families. The M254 and OM654 are proven four‑cylinder units carried over from the W213 generation with 48V and PHEV upgrades. The M256 straight‑six is an established AMG powerplant now offered in PHEV form. As these are very new engines (2023 onward), long‑term reliability data is limited — early indications from the W213 suggest the M254 and OM654 are solid, but all ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The W214 is the sixth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class, launched in May 2023 as the successor to the W213. Built on the MRA2 platform, it is offered in saloon (W214), estate (S214) and All‑Terrain (X214) body styles. The W214 represents a landmark shift for the E‑Class: it is the first generation available exclusively with electrified powertrains — every engine is either a 48V mild hybrid or a plug‑in hybrid. The 2024 model year brought the AMG E 53 HYBRID, a 3.0‑litre straight‑six PHEV producing 449 bhp. The W214 also introduced the MBUX Superscreen dashboard and a 25.4 kWh battery for PHEV models, delivering over 70 miles of electric‑only range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA2 (Modular Rear Architecture) • Predecessor: W213 • Successor: None (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: S214 (estate), X214 (All‑Terrain estate)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W214 E‑Class generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the E 220 d diesel and E 300 e plug‑in hybrid as fleet and business users capitalise on favourable Benefit‑in‑Kind tax rates.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — 197 bhp, 440 Nm of torque, and a 23 bhp ISG boost for seamless off‑the‑line response. This is the fourth generation of the OM654, and it's a proven, refined unit that delivers 56 mpg on the combined WLTP cycle. For UK buyers, this remains the default executive choice.
items.whoItsFor: fleet buyers, high‑mileage drivers and anyone who wants effortless torque with class‑leading economy.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol — 204 bhp, 320 Nm of torque, with a 23 bhp ISG boost. It's smooth, responsive and the most affordable way into W214 ownership. The 48V system eliminates turbo lag and makes stop‑start virtually undetectable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern petrol refinement without the PHEV premium.

items.quote: The most powerful four‑cylinder in the range — 381 bhp system output from a 2.0‑litre turbo petrol and a 129 bhp electric motor. The PHEV system adds significant complexity: battery, cooling, power electronics and the weight of a 25.4 kWh pack. More components means more potential failure points.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want maximum PHEV performance and understand the complexity premium.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre straight‑six — 449 bhp, 560 Nm of torque, paired with a PHEV system. This is a complex, high‑performance powertrain with a twin‑turbo straight‑six, 28.6 kWh battery and electric motor. When something fails — and it will be expensive — only AMG‑trained specialists should touch it.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who understand that 449 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre diesel plug‑in hybrid — 313 bhp system output, 440 Nm of torque, and over 70 miles of electric‑only range. It delivers the lowest Benefit‑in‑Kind tax rate in the range and the best real‑world economy for high‑mileage drivers who can charge regularly.
items.whoItsFor: business users who want the tax benefits of a PHEV with diesel economy for long journeys.

items.quote: The petrol plug‑in hybrid — 313 bhp system output, and the same 25.4 kWh battery as the E 400 e, but with lower running costs. It's often overshadowed by the diesel PHEV and the high‑power E 400 e, but it's the sweet spot for private buyers who want PHEV tax benefits without diesel.
items.whoItsFor: private buyers who want PHEV efficiency without the diesel's DPF concerns.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Entry Point — cheapest W214 to run
Best PHEV — diesel + plug‑in = max efficiency
High‑Power PHEV — more complexity, more cost

economicsRule.title: The W214 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £35,000–£65,000 W214 sits at 5–16% of the car's value — comfortably within the 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the £8,000–£15,000 M256 AMG recon sits at 12–43% against a £35,000 AMG — a repair decision, not a financial one. But the PHEV battery is the wild card: a 25.4 kWh battery replacement could cost £5,000–£10,000 outside warranty. Factor that into your ownership maths.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W214 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 200 (M254.920 MHEV) — simplest powertrain, lowest complexity, cheapest recon.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W214 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 53 HYBRID (M256) — AMG running costs, PHEV complexity, specialist‑only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W214 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 220 d (OM654.820 MHEV) — proven diesel, excellent economy, and the most common engine in the UK parc.

problems.title: OM654 Timing Chain & DPF (E 220 d)
problems.recommendation: On any OM654 W214, maintain the 48V battery health and use genuine Mercedes oil — the 48V ISG places additional strain on the chain. A £1,200–£2,000 chain inspection is far cheaper than a £3,000–£5,500 replacement.

problems.title: M254 48V ISG & Cooling System (Petrol MHEV)
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is the biggest unknown on the W214. Ensure the car has the latest software updates and maintain the 48V battery health — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than £2,500–£5,000 for a full system replacement.

problems.title: PHEV Battery & Cooling System (E 300 e / E 400 e / E 300 de)
problems.recommendation: Before buying any W214 PHEV, check the battery health report from a Mercedes dealer. A £1,000–£2,000 health check is far cheaper than a £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement. Keep the battery between 20% and 80% charge for daily use to maximise lifespan.

problems.title: W214 MBUX Superscreen & Software Glitches
problems.recommendation: Ensure the car has the latest software updates before purchase — £100–£300 now is far cheaper than the £800–£1,500 replacement that follows a hardware failure.

replacementCosts.h2: W214 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M254, OM654 or M256 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines (e.g., Mercedes OM654, BMW B58B30), adjusted for the W214's executive‑class labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages. PHEV battery replacement costs are not included in the above engine costs — budget an additional £5,000–£10,000 for battery replacement outside warranty.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W214 saloon and S214 estate. The saloon and estate share all powertrains, with only minor labour differences for some repairs. The X214 All‑Terrain estate shares the same engine range but is covered on its own Generation Page.
